figures.caption: Figure 1 VoxelCodeBench evaluation pipeline. Given API documentation and a natural language task specification (left), models generate Python code manipulating voxels in 3D space (center). The generated code is executed within VoxelCode, an Unreal Engine based rendering server equipped with the Voxel Plugin 2.0(Careil, 2025), producing visual outputs that can be evaluated by human annotators (right). Our benchmark spans three complexity tiers—from basic geometric primitives to compositional artistic scenes—enabling systematic assessment of spatial reasoning through code generation.

Figure 3 Human annotation interface. Annotators view the prompt and rendered outputs from multiple viewpoints, then rate five dimensions: Has Object, Position Correct, Material Correct, Shape Correct, and Visual Quality (0–10).
Table 4 Error type distribution. Common errors encountered during code execution. Error rate shows percentage of submissions with errors for each model.

Table 1 Main results on VoxelCodeBench. Performance metrics (%) across models. HasObj: output contains visible object. Pos/Mat/Shape: position, material, shape correctness. Look: average visual quality score (0–10). Best results in bold.
Table 2 Performance by task category. Shape correctness (%) and visual quality (Look, 0–10) across three reasoning dimensions. Best per category in bold.

Figure 2 Example outputs using VoxelCode. Representative 3D voxel constructions generated by models across our benchmark, including characters and symbols, geometric and mathematical shapes, artistic animals, objects, vehicles, and natural objects, ranging from simple geometric primitives to complex multi-component objects.

Figure 5 Structured geometric detail generation. Code-based generation produces objects with consistent internal structures (interior views) and fine-grained details (ladders, weapons, floor layouts) that are difficult to achieve with surface-based neural 3D generation methods.

Figure 4 Qualitative comparison acrossmodels, categories, and difficulty levels. Rows correspond to eight evaluated models; columns span three task categories (Symbolic, Geometric, Artistic) at Easy, Medium, and Hard difficulty. Empty cells indicate cases where code execution failed, resulting in no visible output due to errors in the model-generated code.
